 {"id":"asupersync-1n6s","title":"[HTTP] Implement HTTP/1.1 Protocol","description":"# HTTP/1.1 Protocol Implementation

## Overview
Full HTTP/1.1 server and client with keep-alive, pipelining, and chunked encoding.

## Implementation

### Request/Response Types
```rust
pub struct Request<B> {
    pub method: Method,
    pub uri: Uri,
    pub version: Version,
    pub headers: HeaderMap,
    pub body: B,
}

pub struct Response<B> {
    pub status: StatusCode,
    pub version: Version,
    pub headers: HeaderMap,
    pub body: B,
}
```

### HTTP/1.1 Codec
```rust
pub struct Http1Codec {
    state: Http1State,
    max_header_size: usize,
}

enum Http1State {
    ReadingRequestLine,
    ReadingHeaders,
    ReadingBody { content_length: Option<usize>, chunked: bool },
    Complete,
}

impl Decoder for Http1Codec {
    type Item = Request<Bytes>;
    type Error = HttpError;
    
    fn decode(&mut self, src: &mut BytesMut) -> Result<Option<Self::Item>, Self::Error> {
        // Parse request line: GET /path HTTP/1.1\r\n
        // Parse headers until \r\n\r\n
        // Parse body based on Content-Length or Transfer-Encoding
    }
}

impl Encoder<Response<Bytes>> for Http1Codec {
    type Error = HttpError;
    
    fn encode(&mut self, resp: Response<Bytes>, dst: &mut BytesMut) -> Result<(), Self::Error> {
        // Write status line: HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
        // Write headers
        // Write body
    }
}
```

### Server
```rust
pub struct Http1Server<S> {
    service: S,
    config: Http1Config,
}

impl<S> Http1Server<S>
where S: Service<Request<Body>, Response = Response<Body>> {
    pub async fn serve<I>(self, io: I) -> Result<(), HttpError>
    where I: AsyncRead + AsyncWrite + Unpin {
        let mut framed = Framed::new(io, Http1Codec::new());
        
        loop {
            // Read request
            let req = match framed.next().await {
                Some(Ok(req)) => req,
                Some(Err(e)) => return Err(e),
                None => return Ok(()), // Connection closed
            };
            
            // Process request
            let resp = self.service.call(req).await?;
            
            // Send response
            framed.send(resp).await?;
            
            // Check Connection: close header
            if \!should_keep_alive(&resp) {
                break;
            }
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

### Client
```rust
pub struct Http1Client {
    config: Http1Config,
}

impl Http1Client {
    pub async fn request<I>(
        &self,
        io: I,
        req: Request<Body>,
    ) -> Result<Response<Body>, HttpError>
    where I: AsyncRead + AsyncWrite + Unpin {
        let mut framed = Framed::new(io, Http1Codec::new());
        framed.send(req).await?;
        framed.next().await.transpose()?.ok_or(HttpError::ConnectionClosed)
    }
}
```

## Testing
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_http1_roundtrip() {
    let (client_io, server_io) = duplex(8192);
    
    // Server
    let server = tokio::spawn(async move {
        let server = Http1Server::new(|req| async {
            Response::builder()
                .status(200)
                .body(Body::from("Hello"))
                .unwrap()
        });
        server.serve(server_io).await
    });
    
    // Client
    let client = Http1Client::new();
    let req = Request::get("/").body(Body::empty()).unwrap();
    let resp = client.request(client_io, req).await.unwrap();
    
    assert_eq\!(resp.status(), StatusCode::OK);
}
```

 {"id":"asupersync-1sf","title":"Implement ObligationState and ObligationKind enums","description":"# ObligationState and ObligationKind Enums

## Purpose
These types define the two-phase effect system's tracking of linear resources. Obligations are tokens that MUST be resolved (committed or aborted) before their owning region closes.

## ObligationKind
```rust
enum ObligationKind {
    // Channel send permit (reserve capacity, then send)
    SendPermit,
    
    // Message acknowledgment (receive, process, then ack/nack)
    Ack,
    
    // Time-bounded resource lease (must renew or expire)
    Lease,
    
    // In-flight I/O operation (must complete or cancel)
    IoOp,
}
```

## ObligationState
```rust
enum ObligationState {
    // Obligation created, waiting for resolution
    Reserved,
    
    // Obligation fulfilled (effect took place)
    Committed,
    
    // Obligation cleanly cancelled (no effect)
    Aborted,
    
    // ERROR: Obligation lost (holder completed without resolving)
    Leaked,
}
```

## State Transitions

```
Reserved ──────► Committed
    │
    ├──────────► Aborted
    │
    └──────────► Leaked (error state, detected by runtime)
```

All three terminal states (Committed, Aborted, Leaked) are absorbing - once reached, no further transitions.

## Why These Obligation Kinds?

### SendPermit
Two-phase channel send prevents message loss under cancellation:
```rust
let permit = tx.reserve(cx).await?;  // Reserve capacity (cancel-safe)
permit.send(message);                 // Commit: actually send
// OR drop permit → Aborted (capacity released, no message lost)
```

### Ack
Two-phase receive with acknowledgment for reliable messaging:
```rust
let (item, ack) = rx.recv_with_ack(cx).await?;  // Receive but don't dequeue
process(item);
ack.commit();  // Dequeue permanently
// OR drop ack → Aborted (nack, message redelivered)
```

### Lease
Time-bounded resource ownership:
```rust
let lease = resource.lease(duration, cx).await?;
// Use resource...
lease.renew(duration);  // Extend lease
// OR lease expires → Aborted (resource released)
```

### IoOp
In-flight I/O operations bound to region:
```rust
let op = io.submit_read(buffer, cx).await?;  // Submit (region can't close yet)
let result = op.complete().await;             // Wait for completion
// OR op.cancel() → Aborted (I/O cancelled)
```

## ObligationState Semantics

### Reserved
- Obligation exists and must be resolved
- Blocks region close (quiescence requires zero reserved obligations)
- Shows up in obligation registry under holder's region

### Committed
- The effect took place successfully
- E.g., message was sent, ack was committed, I/O completed
- This is the "happy path" resolution

### Aborted
- The effect was cleanly cancelled
- No data loss, no side effects
- Capacity/resources released back
- This is the "safe cancellation" path

### Leaked
- ERROR STATE: Holder completed without resolving
- This indicates a bug in user code or library
- In lab mode: panic immediately
- In prod mode: log error, attempt recovery, continue
- Triggers futurelock detection

## Linear Resource Discipline

Obligations are LINEAR resources in the logic sense:
```
reserve : Cx → Obligation<K, 1>      // Introduce obligation
commit  : Obligation<K, 1> → Cx → () // Eliminate obligation
abort   : Obligation<K, 1> → Cx → () // Eliminate obligation
```

The `1` annotation means "exactly one use." Dropping without explicit resolution is detected and triggers Leaked.

## VASS/Petri Net View

For verification, obligations can be modeled as a vector addition system:
```
marking(r, k) = count of Reserved obligations of kind k in region r

reserve(k) → marking += 1
commit/abort(k) → marking -= 1
region_close requires marking = 0 for all kinds
```

## Implementation Requirements

1. **Both enums must be Copy, Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Eq**
2. **ObligationKind should have Display** for tracing
3. **ObligationState::is_terminal()**: Returns true for Committed/Aborted/Leaked
4. **ObligationState::is_resolved()**: Returns true for Committed/Aborted (not Leaked)
5. **ObligationState::is_error()**: Returns true only for Leaked

## Drop Semantics

When an obligation token is dropped:
- If Reserved → transition to Aborted AND emit trace event
- If already terminal → no-op

In lab mode, dropping a Reserved obligation can optionally panic (configurable).

## Testing Requirements

1. All terminal states are absorbing
2. Leaked is only reachable from Reserved
3. Committed/Aborted are mutually exclusive endpoints
4. Obligation tracking correctly counts per-region

## Invariant Support

### INV-OBLIGATION-BOUNDED
```rust
∀o: O[o].state = Reserved ⟹
    T[O[o].holder].state ∈ {Running, CancelRequested, Cancelling, Finalizing}
```

### INV-OBLIGATION-LINEAR
```rust
∀o: O[o].state ∈ {Committed, Aborted, Leaked} is absorbing
```

### I2: Region close = quiescence
```rust
R[r].state = Closed(_) requires
    ∀o where O[o].region = r: O[o].state ≠ Reserved
```

## Example Usage

```rust
// Two-phase send
let permit: SendPermit<T> = tx.reserve(cx).await?;
// permit.obligation_state() == Reserved

if should_send {
    permit.send(value);  // → Committed
} else {
    drop(permit);  // → Aborted
}

// If cx is cancelled and permit dropped without explicit resolution:
// → Aborted (safe default)
```

## References
- asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md §1.7 (Obligation States)
- asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md §3.4 (Obligations transitions)
- asupersync_plan_v4.md §8 (Two-phase effects + linear obligations)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Defines `ObligationKind` and `ObligationState` per the spec (Reserved/Committed/Aborted/Leaked).
- States are absorbing after resolution (Committed/Aborted/Leaked).
- Implements deterministic, trace-friendly formatting.
- Unit tests cover state ordering (if any), absorbing behavior, and conversions used by the registry.

 {"id":"asupersync-24c","title":"Implement Scope API (user-facing region handle)","description":"# Scope API (User-Facing Region Handle)

## Purpose
`Scope<'r>` is the user-facing handle to a region. It is the API surface that enforces structured concurrency:
- every spawned task is owned by exactly one region
- region close implies quiescence (no live children, finalizers done, obligations resolved)

The lifetime `'r` prevents handles from escaping their region.

## Core Responsibilities
- spawn work (Phase 0: single-thread fiber tier; Phase 1: Send task tier)
- create subregions
- register finalizers
- surface `Cx` capabilities and tracing

## Policy Integration (must exist)
`Scope::region_with_policy` (or equivalent) requires a `Policy` type that defines:
- how to aggregate child outcomes
- how to react to child failures (e.g., fail-fast cancels siblings)

## Key APIs (Sketch)
### spawn
```rust
pub fn spawn<F, T>(&self, f: F) -> JoinHandle<'r, T>
where
    F: Future<Output = T> + 'r,
```

### region
```rust
pub async fn region<F, Fut>(&self, f: F) -> Outcome<()>
where
    F: FnOnce(Scope<'_>) -> Fut,
    Fut: Future<Output = ()>,
```

### finalizers
- `defer_sync` / `defer_async`

### handles
`JoinHandle` supports:
- `join().await -> Outcome<T>`
- `cancel(reason)` (request cancellation; must still drain)

## “Dropping a JoinHandle” Rule
Dropping a handle **does not detach work**. The region still owns the task and will cancel/drain it on region close.

## Example (No stdout/stderr in core)
```rust
root.region(|sub| async move {
    sub.defer_sync(|cx| cx.trace_user("cleanup", TraceData::None));

    let h1 = sub.spawn(async move { 1 });
    let h2 = sub.spawn(async move { 2 });

    let _ = h1.join().await;
    let _ = h2.join().await;
}).await;
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Lifetimes prevent escaping scopes.
- Region close waits for children/finalizers/obligations.
- Policy hooks exist for fail-fast semantics.

## Testing
- Compile-time tests where feasible (no escape).
- Lab E2E scenarios validate nested regions and quiescence.

-{"id":"asupersync-263w","title":"[EPIC-INFRA] Hierarchical Timer Wheel","description":"## Overview

Implement a hierarchical timer wheel for O(1) timer operations, replacing naive linear timer storage. This is a performance-critical optimization for workloads with many concurrent timers.

## Strategic Value

**Problem Solved**: Current timer implementations (if using Vec or BTreeMap) have O(n) or O(log n) cost for timer operations. Servers handling thousands of connections each with timeouts need O(1) operations.

**Why Hierarchical**: Simple timer wheels have limited range. Hierarchical wheels (like Linux kernel) provide O(1) insert/cancel/expire across wide time ranges (milliseconds to hours).

**Production Necessity**: Any serious async runtime needs efficient timer management. This is foundational for production use.

## Algorithm Overview

### Timer Wheel Structure
```
Level 0: 256 slots, 1ms resolution (covers 256ms)
Level 1: 64 slots, 256ms resolution (covers 16.4 seconds)  
Level 2: 64 slots, 16.4s resolution (covers 17.5 minutes)
Level 3: 64 slots, 17.5min resolution (covers 18.6 hours)
```

### Operations
- **Insert**: Hash deadline to appropriate level/slot, O(1)
- **Cancel**: Remove from linked list at slot, O(1) with handle
- **Tick**: Process Level 0, cascade higher levels periodically

### Cascading
When Level 0 wraps, cascade Level 1 slot into Level 0. This amortizes the cost of long-duration timers.

## Design Considerations

### Memory Layout
- Intrusive linked lists for zero-allocation in steady state
- Timer handles store list pointers for O(1) cancel
- Cache-friendly slot arrays

### Precision Trade-offs
- 1ms base resolution is sufficient for network timeouts
- Sub-millisecond needs separate approach (spin loops, etc.)
- Configuration allows tuning resolution vs. memory

### Integration
- Replace current timer storage in time module
- Maintain same API (sleep, timeout, Delay)
- Lab runtime gets separate virtual time wheel

## Acceptance Criteria

1. O(1) insert, cancel, and per-tick expire operations
2. Supports at least 24-hour timer range
3. Memory overhead < 1KB for wheel structure
4. Benchmark shows 10x improvement over BTreeMap for 10K timers
5. No changes to public timer API

## Dependencies

- Standalone module, no major dependencies
- Benefits from good integration test coverage

## Priority Rationale

Ranked #7 because while important for production performance, it is primarily an internal optimization.  {"id":"asupersync-2f7","title":"[Net] Implement UdpSocket","description":"# UDP Socket Implementation

## Overview
Full UDP networking with datagram-oriented operations.

## UdpSocket

```rust
pub struct UdpSocket {
    inner: sys::UdpSocket,
}

impl UdpSocket {
    /// Bind to address
    pub async fn bind(addr: impl ToSocketAddrs) -> io::Result<Self>;
    
    /// Connect to remote (for send/recv)
    pub async fn connect(&self, addr: impl ToSocketAddrs) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Send datagram to specific address
    pub async fn send_to(&self, buf: &[u8], target: impl ToSocketAddrs) -> io::Result<usize>;
    
    /// Receive datagram with source address
    pub async fn recv_from(&self, buf: &mut [u8]) -> io::Result<(usize, SocketAddr)>;
    
    /// Send to connected address
    pub async fn send(&self, buf: &[u8]) -> io::Result<usize>;
    
    /// Receive from connected address
    pub async fn recv(&self, buf: &mut [u8]) -> io::Result<usize>;
    
    /// Peek at datagram without consuming
    pub async fn peek_from(&self, buf: &mut [u8]) -> io::Result<(usize, SocketAddr)>;
    
    /// Get local address
    pub fn local_addr(&self) -> io::Result<SocketAddr>;
    
    /// Get peer address (if connected)
    pub fn peer_addr(&self) -> io::Result<SocketAddr>;
    
    /// Set broadcast
    pub fn set_broadcast(&self, on: bool) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Set multicast loopback
    pub fn set_multicast_loop_v4(&self, on: bool) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Join multicast group
    pub fn join_multicast_v4(&self, multiaddr: Ipv4Addr, interface: Ipv4Addr) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Leave multicast group
    pub fn leave_multicast_v4(&self, multiaddr: Ipv4Addr, interface: Ipv4Addr) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Set TTL
    pub fn set_ttl(&self, ttl: u32) -> io::Result<()>;
}
```

## Datagram Streams

```rust
/// Datagram receive stream
pub struct RecvStream<'a> {
    socket: &'a UdpSocket,
    buf_size: usize,
}

impl Stream for RecvStream<'_> {
    type Item = io::Result<(Vec<u8>, SocketAddr)>;
}

/// Datagram send sink
pub struct SendSink<'a> {
    socket: &'a UdpSocket,
}

impl Sink<(Vec<u8>, SocketAddr)> for SendSink<'_> {
    type Error = io::Error;
}
```

## Cancel-Safety
- send_to/send: atomic datagram, cancel-safe
- recv_from/recv: cancel = datagram discarded (UDP is unreliable anyway)
- connect: cancel-safe (stateless)

## Multicast Support
```rust
impl UdpSocket {
    /// Join multicast group (IPv6)
    pub fn join_multicast_v6(&self, multiaddr: &Ipv6Addr, interface: u32) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Leave multicast group (IPv6)
    pub fn leave_multicast_v6(&self, multiaddr: &Ipv6Addr, interface: u32) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Set multicast TTL
    pub fn set_multicast_ttl_v4(&self, ttl: u32) -> io::Result<()>;
}
```

## Testing
- bind and send_to/recv_from
- connected mode
- multicast join/leave
- broadcast
- concurrent send/recv
- large datagrams

 {"id":"asupersync-2g0","title":"[Net] Implement Unix Sockets (UnixListener, UnixStream, UnixDatagram)","description":"# Unix Socket Implementation

## Overview
Unix domain sockets for local IPC with cancel-correct operations.

## UnixListener

```rust
#[cfg(unix)]
pub struct UnixListener {
    inner: sys::UnixListener,
}

impl UnixListener {
    /// Bind to path
    pub fn bind(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> io::Result<Self>;
    
    /// Bind to abstract address (Linux)
    #[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
    pub fn bind_abstract(name: &[u8]) -> io::Result<Self>;
    
    /// Accept connection
    pub async fn accept(&self) -> io::Result<(UnixStream, SocketAddr)>;
    
    /// Get local address
    pub fn local_addr(&self) -> io::Result<SocketAddr>;
    
    /// Incoming stream
    pub fn incoming(&self) -> Incoming<'_>;
}
```

## UnixStream

```rust
#[cfg(unix)]
pub struct UnixStream {
    inner: sys::UnixStream,
```

 {"id":"asupersync-2j3","title":"Implement test oracle: region_tree_valid invariant checker","description":"## Purpose
Implement a test oracle that verifies the INV-TREE invariant: regions form a proper rooted tree structure where every region (except root) has exactly one parent and is listed in its parent's subregions.

## The Invariant
From asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md §5:
```
∀r ∈ dom(R):
  r = root ∨ (R[r].parent ∈ dom(R) ∧ r ∈ R[R[r].parent].subregions)
```

This invariant ensures:
1. Exactly one root region exists
2. Every non-root region has a valid parent
3. Parent-child relationships are bidirectional (parent.subregions contains child)
4. No cycles exist in the parent relationship

## Oracle Design

```rust
pub struct RegionTreeOracle {
    // Track region creation and parent relationships
    regions: HashMap<RegionId, RegionTreeEntry>,
    root: Option<RegionId>,
}

pub struct RegionTreeEntry {
    pub parent: Option<RegionId>,
    pub subregions: HashSet<RegionId>,
    pub created_at: Time,
}

impl RegionTreeOracle {
    /// Called when region is created
    pub fn on_region_create(&mut self, region: RegionId, parent: Option<RegionId>, time: Time);
    
    /// Called when subregion is added
    pub fn on_subregion_add(&mut self, parent: RegionId, child: RegionId);
    
    /// Verify tree structure invariant
    pub fn check(&self) -> Result<(), RegionTreeViolation>;
}
```

## Violation Detection

```rust
pub enum RegionTreeViolation {
    /// Multiple root regions
    MultipleRoots { roots: Vec<RegionId> },
    
    /// Region has no parent and is not root
    OrphanRegion { region: RegionId },
    
    /// Parent does not exist
    InvalidParent { region: RegionId, claimed_parent: RegionId },
    
    /// Region not in parent's subregions set
    ParentChildMismatch { region: RegionId, parent: RegionId },
    
    /// Cycle detected in parent relationship
    CycleDetected { cycle: Vec<RegionId> },
}
```

## Tree Properties to Verify
1. **Single root**: Exactly one region with parent=None
2. **Parent exists**: For all non-root regions, parent exists in dom(R)
3. **Bidirectional consistency**: r in parent.subregions iff parent.subregions.contains(r)
4. **Acyclicity**: Following parent pointers always reaches root

## Cycle Detection
Use DFS or union-find to detect cycles:
```rust
fn check_acyclic(&self) -> Result<(), Vec<RegionId>> {
    let mut visited = HashSet::new();
    let mut path = Vec::new();
    
    for &region in self.regions.keys() {
        if self.has_cycle_from(region, &mut visited, &mut path)? {
            return Err(path);
        }
    }
    Ok(())
}
```

## Testing the Oracle
1. **Valid tree**: Linear chain of nested regions → passes
2. **Multiple roots**: Two regions with no parent → catches
3. **Orphan region**: Region with non-existent parent → catches
4. **Parent-child mismatch**: Child claims parent but not in subregions → catches
5. **Cycle**: A → B → C → A parent chain → catches

## Relationship to Other Invariants
| Invariant | What It Checks |
|-----------|---------------|
| INV-TREE | **Region tree structure** |
| INV-TASK-OWNED | Task ownership by regions |
| INV-QUIESCENCE | Close semantics |

INV-TREE is foundational - if the tree is malformed, other invariants become meaningless.

## References
- asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md §5: INV-TREE
- asupersync_plan_v4.md §6: Regions and Scopes

## Acceptance Criteria
- Oracle detects violations of the region tree invariants (unique parent, rooted, no cycles).
- Error reporting includes minimal counterexample context (region ids, parent pointers) and trace slice if available.
- Oracle is deterministic and pure (no ambient I/O).
- Unit tests include both valid and invalid synthetic trees.

## Overview
Define the Stream trait and implement fundamental transformation and control flow combinators.

### Step 1: Stream Trait Definition
```rust
use std::pin::Pin;
use std::task::{Context, Poll};

/// Asynchronous iterator producing a sequence of values
pub trait Stream {
    /// Values produced by the stream
    type Item;
    
    /// Attempt to pull out the next value of this stream
    fn poll_next(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Option<Self::Item>>;
    
    /// Hint about remaining items (optional)
    fn size_hint(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>) {
        (0, None)
    }
}

// Auto-impl for Pin<P> where P: DerefMut<Target: Stream>
impl<P> Stream for Pin<P>
where
    P: DerefMut + Unpin,
    P::Target: Stream,
{
    type Item = <P::Target as Stream>::Item;
    
    fn poll_next(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Option<Self::Item>> {
        self.get_mut().as_mut().poll_next(cx)
    }
}

// Impl for Box<S: Stream>
impl<S: Stream + Unpin + ?Sized> Stream for Box<S> {
    type Item = S::Item;
    
    fn poll_next(mut self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Option<Self::Item>> {
        Pin::new(&mut **self).poll_next(cx)
    }
}
```

### Step 2: StreamExt Trait
```rust
/// Extension methods for Stream
pub trait StreamExt: Stream {
    /// Get the next item from the stream
    fn next(&mut self) -> Next<'_, Self>
    where
        Self: Unpin,
    {
        Next { stream: self }
    }
    
    /// Map items with a function
    fn map<T, F>(self, f: F) -> Map<Self, F>
    where
        Self: Sized,
        F: FnMut(Self::Item) -> T,
    {
        Map { stream: self, f }
    }
    
    /// Filter items by predicate
    fn filter<F>(self, predicate: F) -> Filter<Self, F>
    where
        Self: Sized,
        F: FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool,
    {
        Filter { stream: self, predicate }
    }
    
    /// Combined filter and map
    fn filter_map<T, F>(self, f: F) -> FilterMap<Self, F>
    where
        Self: Sized,
        F: FnMut(Self::Item) -> Option<T>,
    {
        FilterMap { stream: self, f }
    }
    
    /// Async map (each item processed with async fn)
    fn then<Fut, F>(self, f: F) -> Then<Self, Fut, F>
    where
        Self: Sized,
        F: FnMut(Self::Item) -> Fut,
        Fut: Future,
    {
        Then { stream: self, f, pending: None }
    }
    
    /// Take first n items
    fn take(self, n: usize) -> Take<Self>
    where
        Self: Sized,
    {
        Take { stream: self, remaining: n }
    }
    
    /// Take while predicate is true
    fn take_while<F>(self, predicate: F) -> TakeWhile<Self, F>
    where
        Self: Sized,
        F: FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool,
    {
        TakeWhile { stream: self, predicate, done: false }
    }
    
    /// Skip first n items
    fn skip(self, n: usize) -> Skip<Self>
    where
        Self: Sized,
    {
        Skip { stream: self, remaining: n }
    }
    
    /// Skip while predicate is true
    fn skip_while<F>(self, predicate: F) -> SkipWhile<Self, F>
    where
        Self: Sized,
        F: FnMut(&Self::Item) -> bool,
    {
        SkipWhile { stream: self, predicate, done: false }
    }
    
    /// Enumerate items with index
    fn enumerate(self) -> Enumerate<Self>
    where
        Self: Sized,
    {
        Enumerate { stream: self, count: 0 }
    }
    
    /// Fuse stream (ensures None after first None)
    fn fuse(self) -> Fuse<Self>
    where
        Self: Sized,
    {
        Fuse { stream: Some(self) }
    }
    
    /// Inspect each item (for debugging)
    fn inspect<F>(self, f: F) -> Inspect<Self, F>
    where
        Self: Sized,
        F: FnMut(&Self::Item),
    {
        Inspect { stream: self, f }
    }
}

impl<S: Stream + ?Sized> StreamExt for S {}
```

### Step 3: Implementation of Combinators
```rust
/// Future for next() method
pub struct Next<'a, S: ?Sized> {
    stream: &'a mut S,
}

impl<S: Stream + Unpin + ?Sized> Future for Next<'_, S> {
    type Output = Option<S::Item>;
    
    fn poll(mut self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Self::Output> {
        Pin::new(&mut *self.stream).poll_next(cx)
    }
}

/// Map combinator
#[pin_project]
pub struct Map<S, F> {
    #[pin]
    stream: S,
    f: F,
}

impl<S, F, T> Stream for Map<S, F>
where
    S: Stream,
    F: FnMut(S::Item) -> T,
{
    type Item = T;
    
    fn poll_next(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Option<T>> {
        let this = self.project();
        match this.stream.poll_next(cx) {
            Poll::Ready(Some(item)) => Poll::Ready(Some((this.f)(item))),
            Poll::Ready(None) => Poll::Ready(None),
            Poll::Pending => Poll::Pending,
        }
    }
    
    fn size_hint(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>) {
        self.stream.size_hint()
    }
}

/// Filter combinator
#[pin_project]
pub struct Filter<S, F> {
    #[pin]
    stream: S,
    predicate: F,
}

impl<S, F> Stream for Filter<S, F>
where
    S: Stream,
    F: FnMut(&S::Item) -> bool,
{
    type Item = S::Item;
    
    fn poll_next(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Option<S::Item>> {
        let mut this = self.project();
        loop {
            match this.stream.as_mut().poll_next(cx) {
                Poll::Ready(Some(item)) => {
                    if (this.predicate)(&item) {
                        return Poll::Ready(Some(item));
                    }
                    // Continue to next item
                }
                Poll::Ready(None) => return Poll::Ready(None),
                Poll::Pending => return Poll::Pending,
            }
        }
    }
}

/// Take combinator
#[pin_project]
pub struct Take<S> {
    #[pin]
    stream: S,
    remaining: usize,
}

impl<S: Stream> Stream for Take<S> {
    type Item = S::Item;
    
    fn poll_next(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Option<S::Item>> {
        let this = self.project();
        
        if *this.remaining == 0 {
            return Poll::Ready(None);
        }
        
        match this.stream.poll_next(cx) {
            Poll::Ready(Some(item)) => {
                *this.remaining -= 1;
                Poll::Ready(Some(item))
            }
            other => other,
        }
    }
    
    fn size_hint(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>) {
        let (lower, upper) = self.stream.size_hint();
        (lower.min(self.remaining), upper.map(|u| u.min(self.remaining)))
    }
}
```

 {"id":"asupersync-ycir","title":"[SUB-EPIC] Unix Domain Sockets (UDS)","description":"# Sub-Epic: Unix Domain Sockets (UDS)

## Purpose

Implement Unix domain socket primitives (UnixListener, UnixStream) for local IPC. This is a critical requirement for rchd (remote compilation helper daemon) which needs efficient local communication.

## Background

### What are Unix Domain Sockets?

Unix domain sockets (UDS) provide inter-process communication on the same host. Unlike TCP/IP:
- No network stack overhead
- Filesystem-based addressing (paths)
- Supports file descriptor passing
- Higher throughput for local communication

### Use Cases for rchd

1. **Daemon control socket**: `/run/rchd.sock` for CLI commands
2. **Build output streaming**: Efficient transfer of compilation results
3. **Worker communication**: Coordinator ↔ worker IPC
4. **SSH agent forwarding**: Standard SSH_AUTH_SOCK protocol

### Socket Types

1. **Stream (SOCK_STREAM)**: Connection-oriented, reliable, ordered (like TCP)
2. **Datagram (SOCK_DGRAM)**: Connectionless, message-oriented (like UDP)
3. **Seqpacket (SOCK_SEQPACKET)**: Connection-oriented, message-oriented

We'll implement Stream first (most common), then Datagram.

## Design

```rust
// Stream sockets (connection-oriented)
pub struct UnixListener { ... }
pub struct UnixStream { ... }

// Datagram sockets (connectionless)
pub struct UnixDatagram { ... }

// Address types
pub use std::os::unix::net::SocketAddr as UnixSocketAddr;
```

## API Design

### UnixListener

```rust
    /// Bind to a filesystem path.
    pub async fn bind<P: AsRef<Path>>(path: P) -> io::Result<Self>;

    /// Get the local socket address.
    pub fn local_addr(&self) -> io::Result<UnixSocketAddr>;
}
```

### UnixStream

```rust
    /// Connect to a path.

    /// Create a connected pair (for testing, parent-child IPC).
    pub fn pair() -> io::Result<(Self, Self)>;
    
    /// Get peer address.
    pub fn peer_addr(&self) -> io::Result<UnixSocketAddr>;
    
    /// Get local address.
    pub fn local_addr(&self) -> io::Result<UnixSocketAddr>;
}

impl AsyncRead for UnixStream { ... }
impl AsyncWrite for UnixStream { ... }
```

### UnixDatagram

```rust
    /// Bind to a path.
    pub async fn bind<P: AsRef<Path>>(path: P) -> io::Result<Self>;
    
    /// Create unbound socket.
    pub fn unbound() -> io::Result<Self>;
    
    /// Send to address.
    pub async fn send_to(&self, buf: &[u8], path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> io::Result<usize>;
    
    /// Receive with sender address.
    pub async fn recv_from(&self, buf: &mut [u8]) -> io::Result<(usize, UnixSocketAddr)>;
    
    /// Connect to specific peer.
    pub async fn connect<P: AsRef<Path>>(&self, path: P) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Send (after connect).
    pub async fn send(&self, buf: &[u8]) -> io::Result<usize>;
```

## File Descriptor Passing

Advanced feature for UDS - can pass open file descriptors between processes:

```rust
impl UnixStream {
    /// Send bytes with attached file descriptors.
    pub async fn send_with_fds(&self, buf: &[u8], fds: &[RawFd]) -> io::Result<usize>;
    
    /// Receive bytes and any attached file descriptors.
    pub async fn recv_with_fds(&self, buf: &mut [u8], fds: &mut Vec<RawFd>) -> io::Result<usize>;
}
```

This uses `sendmsg`/`recvmsg` with `SCM_RIGHTS` ancillary data.

## Filesystem Considerations

1. **Socket file cleanup**: Remove socket file on listener drop
2. **Permission**: Control who can connect via filesystem permissions
3. **Abstract namespace** (Linux): Socket not bound to filesystem (`\0` prefix)

```rust

    pub async fn bind_abstract(name: &[u8]) -> io::Result<Self>;
}
```

## Overview
Full gRPC implementation with all streaming patterns over HTTP/2.

## Implementation

### gRPC Message Framing
```rust
// gRPC message format: 1-byte compressed flag + 4-byte length + payload
pub struct GrpcCodec<C> {
    codec: C,
    max_message_size: usize,
}

impl<C: Codec> Decoder for GrpcCodec<C> {
    type Item = C::Decode;
    type Error = GrpcError;
    
    fn decode(&mut self, src: &mut BytesMut) -> Result<Option<Self::Item>, Self::Error> {
        if src.len() < 5 {
            return Ok(None);
        }
        
        let compressed = src[0] != 0;
        let len = u32::from_be_bytes([src[1], src[2], src[3], src[4]]) as usize;
        
        if len > self.max_message_size {
            return Err(GrpcError::MessageTooLarge);
        }
        
        if src.len() < 5 + len {
            return Ok(None);
        }
        
        src.advance(5);
        let data = src.split_to(len);
        
        // Decompress if needed
        let decoded = if compressed {
            self.codec.decode(&decompress(&data)?)?
        } else {
            self.codec.decode(&data)?
        };
        
        Ok(Some(decoded))
    }
}
```

### Streaming Types
```rust
// Unary: Request -> Response
pub struct Unary<T, B> {
    inner: T,
    _body: PhantomData<B>,
}

// Server streaming: Request -> Stream<Response>
pub struct ServerStreaming<T> {
    inner: T,
}

impl<T: Stream> Stream for ServerStreaming<T> {
    type Item = T::Item;
    fn poll_next(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Option<Self::Item>> {
        self.project().inner.poll_next(cx)
    }
}

// Client streaming: Stream<Request> -> Response
pub struct ClientStreaming<T> {
    sender: mpsc::Sender<T>,
}

impl<T> ClientStreaming<T> {
    pub async fn send(&mut self, item: T) -> Result<(), SendError> {
        self.sender.send(item).await
    }
    
    pub async fn finish(self) -> Result<Response, GrpcError> {
        // Close stream and await response
    }
}

// Bidirectional: Stream<Request> -> Stream<Response>
pub struct Bidirectional<Req, Resp> {
    sender: mpsc::Sender<Req>,
    receiver: mpsc::Receiver<Resp>,
}
```

### Server
```rust
pub struct GrpcServer<S> {
    services: HashMap<String, Box<dyn ServiceHandler>>,
}

impl<S> GrpcServer<S> {
    pub fn add_service<T: NamedService + ServiceHandler>(mut self, svc: T) -> Self {
        self.services.insert(T::NAME.to_string(), Box::new(svc));
        self
    }
    
    pub async fn serve(self, addr: SocketAddr) -> Result<(), GrpcError> {
        let listener = TcpListener::bind(addr).await?;
        loop {
            let (stream, _) = listener.accept().await?;
            let services = self.services.clone();
            tokio::spawn(async move {
                let h2 = H2Connection::accept(stream).await?;
                handle_connection(h2, services).await
            });
        }
    }
}
```

 {"id":"asupersync-ytr","title":"Implement test oracle: deadline_monotone invariant checker","description":"## Purpose
Implement a test oracle that verifies the INV-DEADLINE-MONOTONE invariant: children can never have longer deadlines than their parents.

## The Invariant
From asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md §5:
```
∀r ∈ dom(R), ∀r' ∈ R[r].subregions:
  deadline(R[r']) ≤ deadline(R[r])    // Tighter or equal
```

This ensures budget propagation is correct - a child region cannot escape its parent's deadline.

## Why This Matters
- Prevents orphan work that outlives its parent
- Ensures cancellation can always complete within parent's budget
- Critical for bounded cleanup guarantees

## Oracle Design
```rust
pub struct DeadlineMonotoneOracle {
    region_deadlines: HashMap<RegionId, Option<Time>>,
    parent_map: HashMap<RegionId, RegionId>,
}

impl DeadlineMonotoneOracle {
    pub fn on_region_create(
        &mut self,
        region: RegionId,
        parent: Option<RegionId>,
        budget: &Budget
    );
    
    pub fn on_budget_update(
        &mut self,
        region: RegionId,
        new_budget: &Budget
    );
    
    pub fn check(&self) -> Result<(), DeadlineMonotoneViolation>;
}
```

## Violation Detection
```rust
pub struct DeadlineMonotoneViolation {
    pub child: RegionId,
    pub child_deadline: Option<Time>,
    pub parent: RegionId,
    pub parent_deadline: Option<Time>,
}
```

A violation occurs when:
- Child region has deadline D_c
- Parent region has deadline D_p
- D_c > D_p (child deadline is LATER than parent)

## Integration
- Check on region creation
- Check on budget tightening (should always be valid by construction)
- Lab runtime validates after each step

## Testing
1. Valid: child deadline ≤ parent deadline
2. Invalid: manually construct violation → oracle catches
3. None deadline: unbounded is ≤ bounded (None ≤ Some(T)) - actually None means unbounded which is ≥ any bounded

Actually, the semantics here is:
- None = unbounded = infinity
- Some(T) = bounded to T
- So the check is: child_deadline ≤ parent_deadline where None = ∞

## References
- asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md §5: INV-DEADLINE-MONOTONE
- asupersync_plan_v4.md §3.3: Budget product semiring

## Acceptance Criteria
- Oracle verifies deadline monotonicity: children deadlines are ≤ parent deadlines (or None semantics handled explicitly).
- Produces clear diagnostic output pointing to offending parent/child and their budgets.
- Deterministic and usable in both unit and E2E tests.

 {"id":"asupersync-z454","title":"Implement streaming replay for large traces","description":"## Overview

Implement streaming replay that can process traces incrementally without loading the entire trace into memory, enabling replay of very large traces.

## Problem Statement

Large traces (millions of events) cannot fit in memory. We need streaming support:
1. Streaming write: Write events as they occur, don't buffer all in memory
2. Streaming read: Read and process events one at a time during replay
3. Memory-bounded: Constant memory usage regardless of trace size

## Design

### Streaming TraceWriter

```rust
pub struct StreamingTraceWriter {
    file: BufWriter<File>,
    events_written: u64,
    checksum: Hasher,
}

impl StreamingTraceWriter {
    pub fn create(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> io::Result<Self> {
        let file = File::create(path)?;
        let mut writer = BufWriter::with_capacity(64 * 1024, file);
        
        // Write header placeholder (will update on finish)
        write_header_placeholder(&mut writer)?;
        
        Ok(Self {
            file: writer,
            events_written: 0,
            checksum: Hasher::new(),
        })
    }
    
    /// Write a single event. Does NOT buffer - writes immediately.
    pub fn write_event(&mut self, event: &TraceEvent) -> io::Result<()> {
        let bytes = bincode::serialize(event)
            .map_err(|e| io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidData, e))?;
        
        // Write length-prefixed
        self.file.write_all(&(bytes.len() as u32).to_le_bytes())?;
        self.file.write_all(&bytes)?;
        
        self.checksum.update(&bytes);
        self.events_written += 1;
        
        // Flush periodically for durability
        if self.events_written % 10_000 == 0 {
            self.file.flush()?;
        }
        
        Ok(())
    }
    
    /// Finish writing, update header with event count and checksum.
    pub fn finish(mut self) -> io::Result<TraceFileStats> {
        self.file.flush()?;
        
        // Seek back and update header
        let file = self.file.into_inner()?;
        // ... update header with events_written and checksum
        
        Ok(TraceFileStats {
            events: self.events_written,
            checksum: self.checksum.finalize(),
        })
    }
}
```

### Streaming TraceReader

```rust
pub struct StreamingTraceReader {
    file: BufReader<File>,
    metadata: TraceMetadata,
    events_read: u64,
    total_events: u64,
}

impl StreamingTraceReader {
    pub fn open(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> io::Result<Self> {
        let file = File::open(path)?;
        let mut reader = BufReader::with_capacity(64 * 1024, file);
        
        let header = read_header(&mut reader)?;
        let metadata = read_metadata(&mut reader)?;
        
        Ok(Self {
            file: reader,
            metadata,
            events_read: 0,
            total_events: header.event_count,
        })
    }
    
    /// Read next event. Returns None at end of trace.
    pub fn next_event(&mut self) -> io::Result<Option<TraceEvent>> {
        if self.events_read >= self.total_events {
            return Ok(None);
        }
        
        // Read length prefix
        let mut len_bytes = [0u8; 4];
        self.file.read_exact(&mut len_bytes)?;
        let len = u32::from_le_bytes(len_bytes) as usize;
        
        // Read event bytes
        let mut bytes = vec![0u8; len];
        self.file.read_exact(&mut bytes)?;
        
        let event = bincode::deserialize(&bytes)
            .map_err(|e| io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidData, e))?;
        
        self.events_read += 1;
        Ok(Some(event))
    }
    
    /// Progress through trace (0.0 to 1.0).
    pub fn progress(&self) -> f64 {
        self.events_read as f64 / self.total_events as f64
    }
}

impl Iterator for StreamingTraceReader {
    type Item = io::Result<TraceEvent>;
    
    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> {
        match self.next_event() {
            Ok(Some(event)) => Some(Ok(event)),
            Ok(None) => None,
            Err(e) => Some(Err(e)),
        }
    }
}
```

### Streaming Replayer

```rust
pub struct StreamingReplayer {
    reader: StreamingTraceReader,
    current_event: Option<TraceEvent>,
    runtime: LabRuntime,
}

impl StreamingReplayer {
    /// Create replayer that streams from file.
    pub fn open(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> io::Result<Self> {
        let reader = StreamingTraceReader::open(path)?;
        let runtime = LabRuntime::new_replay_mode(reader.metadata().clone());
        
        Ok(Self {
            reader,
            current_event: None,
            runtime,
        })
    }
    
    /// Peek at next event without consuming.
    pub fn peek(&mut self) -> io::Result<Option<&TraceEvent>> {
        if self.current_event.is_none() {
            self.current_event = self.reader.next_event()?;
        }
        Ok(self.current_event.as_ref())
    }
    
    /// Consume next event and apply to runtime.
    pub fn step(&mut self) -> io::Result<Option<ReplayStep>> {
        let event = match self.peek()? {
            Some(_) => self.current_event.take().unwrap(),
            None => return Ok(None),
        };
        
        let step = self.runtime.apply_replay_event(&event)?;
        Ok(Some(step))
    }
    
    /// Run to completion, streaming events.
    pub fn run_to_end(&mut self) -> io::Result<ReplayResult> {
        while self.step()?.is_some() {
            // Continue
        }
        Ok(self.runtime.finish_replay())
    }
}
```

## Memory Constraints

- StreamingTraceWriter: O(1) memory (just buffer)
- StreamingTraceReader: O(1) memory (just buffer + current event)
- StreamingReplayer: O(runtime state) - not O(trace size)

## Checkpointing (Optional Enhancement)

For very long replays, support checkpointing:

```rust
impl StreamingReplayer {
    /// Save replay state for later resumption.
    pub fn checkpoint(&self) -> io::Result<ReplayCheckpoint> {
        Ok(ReplayCheckpoint {
            event_index: self.reader.events_read,
            runtime_state: self.runtime.snapshot(),
        })
    }
    
    /// Resume from checkpoint.
    pub fn resume(path: impl AsRef<Path>, checkpoint: ReplayCheckpoint) -> io::Result<Self> {
        let mut reader = StreamingTraceReader::open(path)?;
        
        // Skip to checkpoint position
        for _ in 0..checkpoint.event_index {
            reader.next_event()?;
        }
        
        let runtime = LabRuntime::from_snapshot(checkpoint.runtime_state);
        
        Ok(Self {
            reader,
            current_event: None,
            runtime,
        })
    }
}
```
